Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First I need to understand what a process variant is According to the problem it is the ordered sequence of activities for a single case sorted by timestamp So for each case_id I have to collect all its activities in the order they happened based on timestamp to form a sequence Then group cases by these sequences to find unique variants Then count how many cases each variant has pick the top K most frequent ones and finally return all events from cases that are in those top K variants

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Part 1 For each case_id get the ordered sequence of activities So I need to select case_id activity and order them by timestamp Then for each case concatenate the activities in order into a string or an array

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: In SQL to aggregate ordered lists you can use LIST or STRING_AGG

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But since the order matters we need to make sure that within each case the activities are ordered by timestamp before aggregating

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: In DuckDB maybe using the LIST function with an ORDER BY inside the aggregation

Name: Validation - PE
Text: For example SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id Yes DuckDB supports LIST with ORDER BY So that would give for each case_id a list of activities in the order of timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 2 Group by the activity_sequence the list of activities per case count the number of cases which is the count of case_id then order by count descending take top K

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First get all the sequences per case then group by sequence count the cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: WITH case_sequences AS SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT variant COUNT AS case_count FROM case_sequences GROUP BY variant ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT K top_case_ids AS SELECT DISTINCT case_id FROM case_sequences WHERE variant IN SELECT variant FROM variant_counts

Name: Validation - IND
Text: Wait but K is a parameter here The problem says top K variants so the query needs to have K as a variable

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: In DuckDB you can use a parameter like K but maybe the user expects the query to have a placeholder

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Another thing to check the variant is a list array of strings When grouping by variant DuckDB should be able to compare lists for equality So two lists are equal if they have the same elements in the same order

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Yes in DuckDB the list type can be compared for equality so grouping by the list will group cases with the same sequence

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then get all case_ids that belong to these top K variants From the case_sequences the first CTE we need to join with the top variants

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: SELECT case_id FROM case_sequences WHERE variant IN SELECT variant FROM variant_counts

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Finally select all events from the original event_log where case_id is in the list of case_ids from step 3

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: WITH case_variants AS SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id top_variants AS SELECT variant FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT K top_case_ids AS SELECT DISTINCT case_id FROM case_variants WHERE variant IN SELECT variant FROM top_variants SELECT e FROM event_log e WHERE e case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM top_case_ids

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The LIST function in DuckDB according to documentation aggregates the expressions into a list ordered by sort_expression So for each case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp will collect the activities in the order of timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The final query returns all events from the original event_log that belong to cases in the top K variants This seems to satisfy all the requirements

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: The DISTINCT in top_case_ids is redundant because case_variants is grouped by case_id so each case_id appears once

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: WITH case_variants AS SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id top_variants AS SELECT variant FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT K SELECT e FROM event_log e WHERE e case_id IN SELECT cv case_id FROM case_variants cv WHERE cv variant IN SELECT variant FROM top_variants

Name: Validation - PE
Text: In top_variants grouping by variant and ordering by COUNT descending gives the number of cases per variant so it matches the requirement to count how many cases correspond to each variant

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The query correctly orders the activities by timestamp per case because in case_variants we have LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp so for each case the activities are ordered by timestamp
